region_of_origin: Tropical regions; widely produced in Southeast Asia, South Asia, and Latin America

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for dried mango as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for processed fruit is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of dried mango is approximately 30g (a small handful), providing around 94 kcal and 17.5g of sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Dried Mango — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for dried mango (F04030022). Saturated fat not reported by HPB; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for dried mango (FDC ID 168163) to supply saturated fat and additional micronutrient values.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entry for dried mango
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 51 (low) for dried mango is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2021). Glycemic load per 30g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (51 × 21.37) / 100 = 10.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sugar content (58.3g/100g) driving insulin-mediated sodium retention; sodium at 49mg/100g is moderate but the sugar load is the primary cardiovascular concern
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit dried mango to no more than 15–20g per occasion due to the very high sugar concentration (58.3g/100g), which promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and elevated blood pressure per AHA 2018 dietary guidelines; choose unsweetened varieties and pair with protein or healthy fat to blunt the glycaemic response.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar content of 58.3g/100g far exceeds the 20g/100g glycaemic ceiling for diabetes management; concentrated natural and added sugars cause rapid and significant postprandial blood glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dried mango is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its extremely high sugar content (58.3g/100g); if consumed, restrict to a maximum of 10–15g per serving, pair with protein or fat to slow glucose absorption, and monitor postprandial blood glucose closely; fresh mango in small portions (80–100g) is a substantially better alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sugar content (58.3g/100g) associated with elevated triglycerides, LDL-C, and systemic inflammation; dietary fibre (9.8g/100g) and potassium (279mg/100g) provide partial cardioprotective offset
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume dried mango sparingly (≤15g per occasion) given the very high sugar content (58.3g/100g) which is associated with elevated triglycerides and LDL cholesterol; the 9.8g/100g dietary fibre and 279mg/100g potassium provide modest cardioprotective benefit but are insufficient to offset the sugar-driven cardiovascular risk at typical snack portions.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sugar content of 58.3g/100g exceeds the 15g/100g RA threshold, promoting pro-inflammatory cytokine activity; beta-carotene and vitamin C present but insufficient to offset sugar-driven inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit dried mango due to its very high sugar content (58.3g/100g), which promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine production; fresh mango provides anti-inflammatory carotenoids and vitamin C without the concentrated sugar load and is the preferred alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dried mango is a processed fruit product made by dehydrating fresh mango (Mangifera indica) to remove most of its moisture content, resulting in a chewy, intensely sweet-tart snack with a bright yellow-orange colour. The dehydration process concentrates the natural sugars of fresh mango dramatically — from approximately 14g/100g in fresh fruit to 58.3g/100g in the dried form — making it one of the highest-sugar dried fruits available. Per 100g, dried mango provides 314 kcal, 1.25g protein, 0.55g fat, 71.22g carbohydrate, 58.3g sugar, 9.8g dietary fibre, and 49mg sodium. The high fibre content (9.8g/100g) partially moderates the glycaemic response, contributing to a relatively low glycemic index of 51, though the glycemic load per 30g serving is still approximately 10.9 — clinically meaningful for individuals managing blood glucose. Dried mango retains useful micronutrients including potassium (279mg/100g), vitamin C (21.1mg/100g), vitamin A (54mcg RAE/100g), and iron (1.1mg/100g). Clinically, dried mango is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or those managing blood glucose due to the extreme sugar concentration. It should be consumed with caution by individuals with hypertension and cardiovascular disease. It is plant-based, vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. Commonly consumed as a snack, it is widely available across Southeast Asia, South Asia, and globally as a packaged snack product.
common_user_queries: How much sugar is in dried mango?
Is dried mango good for weight loss?
Is dried mango high in fibre?
Dried mango vs fresh mango nutrition
Can I eat dried mango with hypertension?
